name: strawberry peach vinaigrette
instructions:
MACERATE THE GARLIC: microplane garlic and macerate in white wine vinegar and balsamic vinegar for ~15 minutes. This softens the raw garlic edge before it meets the fruit.

PREP THE PEACH: grate, brunoise, or food-process a peach into a fine pulp. Skin can stay on.

CRUSH THE STRAWBERRIES: crush a small handful of strawberries by hand (or in a mortar) until you reach the desired sweetness and pulp level. No need to grate them — uneven crushed strawberry is fine. Taste as you go: peaches are not always ripe enough on their own, and the strawberries are what fill in the missing sweetness when they aren't.

COMBINE: stir the peach pulp and crushed strawberries into the macerated vinegar-garlic. Add olive oil, black pepper, and chiffonaded mint leaves (and/or basil). Mix.

FINISH: the result is pulpy and thick — that's the intended texture, not a defect. Serve over the salad.
notes:
Built on the same scaffolding as the tomato vinaigrette (samin nosrat) — microplaned-garlic + vinegar maceration, then a grated/crushed fruit pulp folded in, finished with olive oil and black pepper. But everything downstream of the maceration is swapped to a sweet-fruit register.

DIFFERENCES FROM TOMATO VINAIGRETTE:
- WHITE WINE VINEGAR (not red): keeps the dressing color clean and lets the strawberry pink come through. Red wine vinegar would mud the color and fight the fruit sweetness.
- BALSAMIC ALONGSIDE: the balsamic gives a slight syrupy depth that suits the sweet-fruit register.
- NO ONION, NO SALT: both would pull the dressing back toward savory territory. The fruit doesn't want them.
- PEACH IN PLACE OF TOMATO: grated/processed peach is the structural fruit. Tomato vinaigrette uses grated tomato; this is the sweet analog.
- STRAWBERRY AS SWEETNESS RESERVE: crushed strawberries fill in when the peach isn't sweet or ripe enough. Adjust quantity to taste rather than to a measurement.
- MINT AND/OR BASIL: chiffonaded fresh leaves — see the herbs and greens article on chiffonading mint. Mint pairs naturally with both peach and strawberry; basil works alongside or instead. Both are primary; pick one or use both.

PULPY AND THICK IS THE INTENT: this dressing doesn't pour like a vinaigrette — it spoons. The peach and strawberry pulp gives it body. Don't try to thin it with more vinegar or oil; that takes you away from the dish it's designed to dress.

SALAD PAIRINGS:
- Caprese base with spring mix and mozzarella (the canonical use — substitutes the tomato vinaigrette in a caprese salad with a fruit-forward version).
- Goat cheese + toasted walnuts on greens.
- Brie on greens (works well with the sweet-pulp profile).
Any of these capitalize on the fruit-vinegar profile against a soft fresh cheese.
